items.q: What did Murdock claim about the nuclear family?
items.correct: It is universal and performs four essential functions in all societies.
items.misconception: It is the only valid family form and others are dysfunctional.
items.random_wrong: It is a recent invention of capitalism.
items.why: Students confuse Murdock's empirical claim (universality) with a normative claim (it SHOULD be this way). His argument was descriptive functionalism, later critiqued by feminists and historians.

items.q: Why do postmodernists reject Murdock's universal nuclear family thesis?
items.correct: Family forms are now diverse and fluid - no single type dominates.
items.misconception: Because nuclear families no longer exist at all.
items.random_wrong: Because Marx proved families are exploitative.
items.why: Students overstate the postmodern position. Nuclear families still exist; the point is that they are one form among many (lone-parent, reconstituted, same-sex, cohabiting).

items.q: What does Parsons mean by 'meritocracy' in education?
items.correct: Rewards and roles are allocated based on individual achievement.
items.misconception: Everyone gets equal outcomes regardless of effort.
items.random_wrong: Only middle-class students can succeed.
items.why: Students confuse meritocracy with equality of outcome. Meritocracy is equality of OPPORTUNITY - rewards differ based on merit. Marxists argue it's a myth.

items.q: What is the 'hidden curriculum'?
items.correct: The unspoken norms, values, and behaviours taught implicitly in schools.
items.misconception: Secret lessons teachers deliberately hide from students.
items.random_wrong: Topics removed from the syllabus.
items.why: Students take 'hidden' too literally as a conspiracy. It refers to things learned implicitly (punctuality, hierarchy, obedience) rather than explicitly taught content.

items.q: Why did Durkheim see crime as functional?
items.correct: It reinforces collective values through punishment and reaction.
items.misconception: Because criminals provide a useful service.
items.random_wrong: Because crime makes society wealthier.
items.why: Students miss that 'functional' means 'serves a purpose for social cohesion'. Crime clarifies moral boundaries when society reacts to it - the function is the REACTION, not the act.

items.q: What is the Marxist view on white-collar crime?
items.correct: It is widespread but under-policed because law serves ruling-class interests.
items.misconception: It does not really happen - only the working class commit crime.
items.random_wrong: It is treated more harshly than street crime.
items.why: Students misread Marxist theory as denying working-class crime exists. The Marxist point is about SELECTIVE ENFORCEMENT - all classes commit crime, but only some get prosecuted.

items.q: What does the chivalry thesis claim?
items.correct: Women are treated more leniently by the criminal justice system.
items.misconception: Women never commit crime.
items.random_wrong: Men are oppressed by chivalrous laws.
items.why: Students think the thesis denies female criminality. It actually argues women's offences are UNDER-RECORDED because police/courts treat them paternalistically - it's about treatment, not behaviour.

items.q: Why do positivists prefer quantitative methods?
items.correct: They produce objective, reliable, generalisable data resembling natural science.
items.misconception: Because numbers are easier to collect than words.
items.random_wrong: Because they hate qualitative research.
items.why: Students reduce the choice to convenience. Positivism is a philosophical commitment to studying society scientifically - the preference is about EPISTEMOLOGY, not ease.

items.q: What do interpretivists seek when researching society?
items.correct: Verstehen - understanding actors' subjective meanings.
items.misconception: Statistical patterns and laws.
items.random_wrong: To manipulate variables experimentally.
items.why: Students apply positivist goals to interpretivism. Interpretivists (Weber) reject the natural-science model - humans interpret and give meaning, so research must access meaning, not measure behaviour.

items.q: What is cultural capital according to Bourdieu?
items.correct: Cultural knowledge, tastes, and skills that confer advantage in education and society.
items.misconception: The amount of money parents spend on culture.
items.random_wrong: Government funding for the arts.
items.why: Students think 'capital' means money. Bourdieu used it metaphorically - cultural capital is non-economic (knowing how to 'play the game' in middle-class institutions like schools).

items.q: How does labelling theory explain ethnic differences in achievement?
items.correct: Teacher stereotypes lead to differential expectations and self-fulfilling prophecies.
items.misconception: Different ethnic groups have different innate abilities.
items.random_wrong: Schools have no influence on outcomes.
items.why: Students drift toward biological explanations. Labelling theory is sociological - it locates the cause in social INTERACTION (teacher perceptions shaping pupil identities and outcomes).

items.q: What does secularisation theory claim?
items.correct: Religion is declining in social significance in modern societies.
items.misconception: Religion has completely disappeared from society.
items.random_wrong: Religion is becoming more important globally.
items.why: Students overstate secularisation as total disappearance. The thesis is about declining INFLUENCE on institutions and lives, not zero religiosity. Critics like Davie note 'believing without belonging'.

items.q: What does Ritzer's 'McDonaldisation' refer to?
items.correct: The spread of fast-food principles (efficiency, calculability, predictability, control) across society.
items.misconception: The literal spread of McDonald's restaurants worldwide.
items.random_wrong: Americans eating more burgers.
items.why: Students take the term literally. Ritzer used McDonald's as a metaphor for Weberian rationalisation - it's about ORGANISATIONAL PRINCIPLES, not the brand itself.

items.q: How does radical feminism differ from liberal feminism?
items.correct: Radical feminists see patriarchy as the primary system of oppression requiring fundamental change.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same with minor differences.
items.random_wrong: Radical feminists oppose all women's rights.
items.why: Students treat feminisms as one bloc. Liberal feminists seek reform within existing systems; radical feminists see patriarchy as inherent to those systems and demand structural transformation.

items.q: What is primary socialisation?
items.correct: Early learning of norms and values, primarily within the family.
items.misconception: The most important socialisation, happening in school.
items.random_wrong: Learning facts in primary school.
items.why: Students confuse 'primary' with primary school. Primary = first/early (family); secondary = later (school, peers, media). The terms describe ORDER, not importance or location.

items.q: What do feral children studies suggest about socialisation?
items.correct: Human behaviour and language depend heavily on social interaction during development.
items.misconception: Humans are entirely determined by biology.
items.random_wrong: Humans need no social input to develop normally.
items.why: Students sometimes invert the conclusion. Feral children (Genie, Oxana) lacked normal language and social skills, supporting the sociological view that humans are MADE social, not born so.

items.q: What is the correspondence principle?
items.correct: School structures mirror workplace structures, preparing pupils for capitalist labour.
items.misconception: Schools teach pupils to correspond with each other through letters.
items.random_wrong: School subjects must match employer demands.
items.why: Students misread 'correspondence' as communication. Bowles and Gintis meant MIRRORING - hierarchy, rewards, alienation in school correspond to the same in work.

items.q: What is the 'dark figure' of crime?
items.correct: The amount of crime that is unreported or unrecorded.
items.misconception: Crimes committed at night.
items.random_wrong: Organised crime by criminal masterminds.
items.why: Students take 'dark' literally. It's a methodological concept - official statistics undercount crime because much goes unreported (victims fearful) or unrecorded (police discretion).

items.q: According to Becker, what makes an act deviant?
items.correct: Being labelled as deviant by others - deviance is in the reaction, not the act.
items.misconception: Breaking a law makes any act deviant.
items.random_wrong: Acts are objectively deviant regardless of context.
items.why: Students assume deviance is inherent in the act. Becker's point is that deviance is socially constructed - the same act may or may not be labelled deviant depending on who does it and who reacts.

items.q: What is the postmodern view of identity?
items.correct: Identities are fluid, fragmented, and constructed through consumption and choice.
items.misconception: Identity is fixed by class, gender, and ethnicity at birth.
items.random_wrong: Everyone has the same identity in postmodern society.
items.why: Students apply structural sociology to postmodernism. Postmodernists explicitly REJECT fixed structural identities - we 'pick and mix' identities through lifestyle choices.

items.q: What is a moral panic according to Cohen?
items.correct: Disproportionate media reaction creating exaggerated public concern about a group or behaviour.
items.misconception: Genuine widespread fear about a real, serious threat.
items.random_wrong: Religious leaders condemning immorality.
items.why: Students miss the 'disproportionate' element. The concept is critical of media amplification - it's about REACTION exceeding the threat, not about whether the underlying issue is real.

items.q: What is the structure-agency debate?
items.correct: Whether human behaviour is shaped more by social structures or individual choice.
items.misconception: Whether buildings or workers are more important to society.
items.random_wrong: A debate about government structures.
items.why: Students take 'structure' to mean physical structures. In sociology, structure = enduring patterns/institutions; agency = individual capacity to act. Giddens proposes both interact (structuration).

items.q: What does Murray's 'underclass' thesis argue?
items.correct: A distinct group develops a culture of welfare dependency and anti-social behaviour.
items.misconception: All working-class people are an underclass.
items.random_wrong: Wealthy elites secretly control society.
items.why: Students conflate underclass with working class. Murray's controversial New Right thesis specifically targets a (supposedly) culturally distinct group beneath the working class - critics reject the concept entirely.

items.q: What is Young and Willmott's symmetrical family thesis?
items.correct: That modern families have moved toward more shared, joint roles between partners — domestic and economic responsibilities increasingly shared, in contrast to segregated traditional roles.
items.misconception: The symmetrical family means men and women now do exactly equal amounts of housework.
items.random_wrong: The symmetrical family means families with the same number of boys and girls.
items.why: Young and Willmott argued for a TREND toward symmetry, not actual equality — feminist critics (Oakley) showed the data overstated change. Students treat 'symmetrical' as full equality or as physical symmetry.

items.q: What is a reconstituted family?
items.correct: A family formed when partners with children from previous relationships come together — a 'blended' or step-family — one of several family forms recognised in family diversity literature.
items.misconception: A reconstituted family is one that has been put back together after divorce.
items.random_wrong: A reconstituted family is a family living in temporary housing.
items.why: Reconstituted refers to the new structure of combining partners and children from different prior unions, not reuniting an original family. Students misread 'reconstituted' as restoration rather than reformation.

items.q: What do feminists mean by the 'dark side' of family life?
items.correct: The reality that families can be sites of inequality, exploitation and violence — particularly affecting women and children — challenging functionalist portrayals of the family as universally beneficial.
items.misconception: The 'dark side' refers to families who live in poverty.
items.random_wrong: The 'dark side' is a metaphor about families who don't communicate.
items.why: The feminist critique specifically targets gendered inequalities and domestic abuse hidden behind the functionalist 'warm bath' image. Students miss the structural critique and reduce it to general hardship.

items.q: What is the difference between material and cultural deprivation?
items.correct: Material deprivation refers to lack of physical/economic resources (housing, books, food); cultural deprivation refers to lack of skills, values and norms (e.g. parental attitudes to education) that are claimed to support achievement.
items.misconception: Material and cultural deprivation are two names for the same poverty.
items.random_wrong: Material deprivation is about a lack of clothing; cultural deprivation is about not visiting museums.
items.why: Material is structural-economic; cultural deprivation theory is contested (criticised as blaming families). Students conflate them or trivialise cultural deprivation, missing how each explanation locates underachievement differently.

items.q: What is a self-fulfilling prophecy in education?
items.correct: When teachers' expectations (often based on labels) influence pupil behaviour and achievement — pupils labelled as 'bright' tend to do better, those labelled 'thick' worse, regardless of underlying ability (Rosenthal and Jacobson).
items.misconception: A self-fulfilling prophecy is when a student predicts their own grade correctly.
items.random_wrong: A self-fulfilling prophecy is when teachers predict the future accurately.
items.why: The mechanism is the LABEL changing teacher behaviour, which changes pupil outcomes — not students predicting themselves. Students miss the causal chain (label → interaction → outcome) that makes the concept analytically powerful.

items.q: What is Merton's strain theory of crime?
items.correct: Crime results from a mismatch between cultural goals (success, wealth) and legitimate means of achieving them — those who can't access legitimate means may innovate (crime), ritualise, retreat or rebel.
items.misconception: Strain theory says people commit crime because they are stressed.
items.random_wrong: Strain theory says criminals have psychological strain disorders.
items.why: 'Strain' is structural (gap between goals and means), not psychological stress. Students take the everyday meaning of strain. Merton's theory locates crime in social structure, specifically blocked opportunities.

items.q: Why do positivists and interpretivists prefer different methods?
items.correct: Positivists treat sociology like natural science, seeking objective measurable patterns — preferring quantitative methods. Interpretivists believe humans construct meaning, requiring qualitative methods to access subjective experience.
items.misconception: Positivists like numbers and interpretivists like words — that's the whole difference.
items.random_wrong: Both positivists and interpretivists prefer experiments above all else.
items.why: The choice flows from EPISTEMOLOGICAL commitments about what sociology can know, not preference for data types. Students reduce the debate to numbers vs words, missing the philosophical underpinnings.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity in research?
items.correct: Reliability is whether a method produces consistent results when repeated; validity is whether it actually measures what it claims to. A method can be reliable but invalid, or valid but unreliable.
items.misconception: Reliable and valid mean the same thing — that the research is good.
items.random_wrong: Reliable research is published in journals; valid research isn't.
items.why: The distinction is crucial. A bathroom scale that always reads 5kg too high is reliable but invalid. Students conflate them as general quality, missing the methodological precision each term carries.

items.q: What is ethnography in sociology?
items.correct: An in-depth qualitative method involving prolonged immersion in a community or setting to understand it from the inside — yielding rich descriptive data about culture, meaning and interaction.
items.misconception: Ethnography is the same as a written survey of a community.
items.random_wrong: Ethnography is the study of different ethnic groups only.
items.why: Ethnography is about method (immersive observation), not topic. Students confuse it with surveys (different method) or limit it to ethnicity (different topic). Ethnographers study workplaces, gangs, hospitals — any setting.

items.q: When is snowball sampling appropriate?
items.correct: When studying hard-to-reach or hidden populations (drug users, undocumented migrants) where a sampling frame doesn't exist — participants recruit further participants. It sacrifices representativeness for access.
items.misconception: Snowball sampling is a type of random sampling.
items.random_wrong: Snowball sampling is only used in winter research.
items.why: Snowball sampling is the opposite of random — it relies on social networks, so participants share characteristics. Students confuse it with probability methods, missing why it is used (access) and its limit (representativeness).

items.q: How did Marx define social class?
items.correct: By relationship to the means of production: the bourgeoisie own productive resources; the proletariat sell their labour. Class is a structural relationship, not just income level.
items.misconception: Marx defined class by how much money people earn.
items.random_wrong: Marx defined class by occupational status like the Registrar General.
items.why: Marx's definition is RELATIONAL (to means of production), not gradational (by income). A highly paid worker is still proletariat; a struggling small business owner can be petite bourgeoisie. Students apply modern income-based thinking to Marx.

items.q: What are Weber's three dimensions of stratification?
items.correct: Class (economic position in markets), status (social honour/prestige), and party (organised power) — Weber argued these can vary independently, unlike Marx's economic-determinism view.
items.misconception: Weber agreed with Marx that class is purely economic.
items.random_wrong: Weber's three dimensions are upper, middle and working class.
items.why: Weber's pluralist analysis is a deliberate alternative to Marx. A poor priest can have high status; rich criminals may have low status. Students collapse Weber into Marx or into traditional three-tier class talk.

items.q: What is ethnocentrism?
items.correct: Judging other cultures by the standards of one's own — assuming one's own cultural practices and values are normal or superior. It is a methodological problem in social research as well as a social phenomenon.
items.misconception: Ethnocentrism is the same as racism.
items.random_wrong: Ethnocentrism means concentrating on your own ethnic group only.
items.why: Ethnocentrism involves judgement using one's own framework, while racism asserts inherent inferiority. Researchers may inadvertently be ethnocentric without being racist. Students treat the two as synonyms, missing the methodological dimension.
